g.data-narration: Welcome to Comparison Counting Sort - a powerful non-comparison based sorting algorithm with linear time complexity.
g.text: university 2022 Scheme - CSE • Non-Comparison Based Sorting

g.data-narration: Counting Sort determines element positions by counting how many elements are smaller, using element values as array indices.
g.text: A non-comparison based sorting algorithm that sorts by counting
how many elements are smaller than each element.
Key Difference: Uses element VALUES as indices, not for comparisons!

g.data-narration: The algorithm uses two key parameters: n for elements and k for range. It requires a count array and output array.
Counting Sort operates in three distinct phases: counting occurrences, computing cumulative sums, and building output.
Phase 1: We create a count array of size k+1 and traverse input, incrementing count at each element's value index.
g.text: Input: [2, 5, 3, 2, 4, 5, 1, 3] | Max = 5, so count array size = 6
After counting: [0, 1, 2, 2, 1, 2] → value 2 appears twice, value 5 appears twice, etc.

g.data-narration: Phase 2 transforms counts to cumulative sums - each position shows total elements ≤ that value. This determines final positions.
g.text: Transform count array into cumulative counts - tells us where each element should go

g.data-narration: Phase 3 traverses input from right-to-left, placing each element at position = count[value] - 1, then decrements count.
g.text: Traverse input from RIGHT to LEFT (for stability) | position = count[value] - 1

g.data-narration: Let's trace through the complete example with the input array [2,5,3,2,4,5,1,3] to see how sorting works step by step.
g.text: ★ Note: Two 5s maintain order (positions 7→6), two 2s maintain order - this is STABILITY!

g.data-narration: Time complexity is O(n + k) - linear in both input size and range. Space complexity is O(n + k) for auxiliary arrays.
g.text: ✓ Efficient when k = O(n) or k < n² | ✗ Inefficient when k >> n (large space)

g.data-narration: Counting Sort achieves stability by traversing the input from right-to-left, ensuring equal elements maintain their relative order.
g.text: Stability = equal elements maintain relative order from input to output
Right-to-left ensures A (at pos 0) is placed after C (at pos 2) → A comes BEFORE C in output

g.data-narration: Counting Sort offers guaranteed linear time, stability, and simplicity, but is limited to integers and requires advance knowledge of maximum value.
g.text: ✓ Guaranteed O(n+k) - no worst case degradation
✗ Requires advance knowledge of max value

g.data-narration: Use Counting Sort when the range k is small and close to n, such as sorting marks, ages, or any bounded integer data.
Compare Counting Sort with Quick Sort, Merge Sort, and Heap Sort in terms of time, space, stability, and in-place sorting.
